Data Viz 2: Mapping Areas and Context

Learning Objectives

By the end of this practical lab you will be able to: * Map the attributes of areas including contextual points and lines using base R * Creating layered maps using ggplot2 * Creating layered maps using the tmap package

Making maps in R

Like most analysis task within R there are a few different ways in we can make maps. In this practical lab we will introduce various ways in which we can map the attribute of areas and contextualize these to build a richer cartography. We will illustrate this through base R, and a series of packages including ggplot2 and tmap.

Choropleth mapping in base R

Before we can create a map in R, we first need to import some spatial data. We will read in two shapefiles, the first containing polygons that will later be used to create a choropleth map, and the second some street segments that will be used to provide context.

#Load required package
library(sf)

# check wd
getwd()
## [1] "E:/Dropbox (Personal)/Auckland/Teaching/sem 1 2025/771/lab 1/Lab 1 _ 2024/2025"
setwd("E:\\Dropbox (Personal)\\Auckland\\Teaching\\sem 1 2025\\771\\lab 1\\Lab 1 _ 2024\\2025")

#Read polygons (creates a simple features object)
LSOA <- st_read("./data/E06000042.shp")
## Reading layer `E06000042' from data source 
##   `E:\Dropbox (Personal)\Auckland\Teaching\sem 1 2025\771\lab 1\Lab 1 _ 2024\2025\data\E06000042.shp' 
##   using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
## Simple feature collection with 152 features and 12 fields
## Geometry type: POLYGON
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: 476285.3 ymin: 230902.3 xmax: 496530.4 ymax: 256158.2
## Projected CRS: Transverse_Mercator
#Read lines (creates a simple features object)
roads <- st_read("./data/Road.shp")
## Reading layer `Road' from data source 
##   `E:\Dropbox (Personal)\Auckland\Teaching\sem 1 2025\771\lab 1\Lab 1 _ 2024\2025\data\Road.shp' 
##   using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
## Simple feature collection with 12813 features and 1 field
## Geometry type: MULTILINESTRING
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: 476776.1 ymin: 230934.7 xmax: 496208.8 ymax: 256127
## Projected CRS: Transverse_Mercator

We can can use the plot() function which is built into base R to show the outlines of the polygons contained within the “LSOA” object.

plot(st_geometry(LSOA))

This map shows the Lower Layer Super Output Area boundaries for the city of Milton Keynes, UK. The attributes of the data frame are the overall and domain scores for the 2015 Index of Multiple Deprivation.

We will shade in this map using the overall IMD score which is stored in the column “imd_rank”. There are a total of 152 values.

LSOA$imd_rank<- as.numeric(LSOA$imd_rank)
LSOA$imd_rank
##   [1] 11648 25604 22671  8671 10843 11207 12012  3373 20067  7551  9137 22727
##  [13] 25119 12911 11061  6095 10851 20505  6437 15455 18551  7911 22588 27851
##  [25] 31225 16778  8497 22734 20954  6565   752  2839  2770 18299  9368 23993
##  [37] 29156 24245 21270 18728 16220 24263 14368 24839 25995 23522 25124 29853
##  [49] 14061 24259 22557 13307 13909 31203 14125 27752 21883 15621 11812 27468
##  [61] 25571 24412 31672 21115 22891 24975 32370 22815 11324 27378 15427 27970
##  [73] 24601 28917 27549 30837 28489 25950 24948 31850 25107 32657 27560 28558
##  [85] 25444 21660 17228  7774 24838  4724 16243 25828 12185  2721  2055 21149
##  [97] 17150 27271 25171 30119 19675 21053  5817 20887 20572 13906 23128 20255
## [109] 20793 32082 21992 28958 10631 23268 21406 18398 27979  9328  5327 15527
## [121] 12061 13869  3727 21063 15044   825  1058   771  1544  9739  4201  4959
## [133]  8465  6155  5279 19975 24541 27256 17521 25346 29805 20147 21800 28875
## [145] 27058 27277 30067 25308 28683 15305 20663 17546

The first step is to find suitable breakpoints for the data contained in the imd_rank column. The continuous data needs to be assigned into categories so different colors can be applied on a choropleth map. There are numerous ways of doing this such as jenks, standard deviations or equal intervals. In this example we use a new function classIntervals() from the “classInt” package to find the ranges needed to divide the the imd_rank into five categories. In this example we use the style “fisher” to specify jenks as the break point.

#install the package if needed
#install.packages("classInt")
# Load package
library(classInt)
#Create breaks
breaks <- classIntervals(LSOA$imd_rank, n = 5, style = "fisher")

If we print the object created by the classIntervals() function, a summary is printed showing you what breaks have been assigned, and how many areas are within these ranges.

breaks
## style: fisher
##   one of 20,811,575 possible partitions of this variable into 5 classes
##        [752,7058)      [7058,13109)   [13109,19201.5) [19201.5,26526.5) 
##                21                22                24                54 
##   [26526.5,32657] 
##                31

We need to choose some colors that we will assign to each of the break points in the data. We will now use another package called “RColorBrewer” which provides a series of color pallets that are suitable for mapping. You can have a look at the color pallets online: http://colorbrewer2.org. Each of these pallets are named; and you can see all the available pallets as follows.

#install the package if needed
#install.packages("RColorBrewer")
#Load package
library(RColorBrewer)
#Display all pallets
display.brewer.all()

We will then choose six colors from the pallet “YlOrRd”, and print them to the terminal. You will see that the colors are stored as hex values.

my_colours <- brewer.pal(6, "YlOrRd")
my_colours
## [1] "#FFFFB2" "#FED976" "#FEB24C" "#FD8D3C" "#F03B20" "#BD0026"

We can then use the function findColours() to select the appropriate color for each of the numbers we intend to map, depending on where these fit within the break points we calculated.

colours_to_map <- findColours(breaks, my_colours)

We can then create a basic map using this list of colors and the plot() function again.

plot(LSOA["imd_rank"],col=colours_to_map)

We might also want to create a map without the borders, and this can be controlled with an additional parameter which is set to “NA”

plot(LSOA["imd_rank"],col=colours_to_map,border = NA)

We can also add additional layers onto the map using a further parameter (“add”) which is set to “TRUE”. Without the “add=TRUE”, every time plot() is called, the previous plot is replaced. Two further parameters are used, “col” to specify the line color, and “lwd” the line width.

plot(LSOA["imd_rank"],col=colours_to_map,border = NA, reset = FALSE)
plot(st_geometry(roads),col="#6B6B6B",lwd=0.3, add=TRUE)

#note that you need to run both cells at the same time, otherwise you will get an error.

Another feature that is very common to see on a map is a legend which tells you what values the colors used on the map correspond to.

# Plot choropleth
plot(LSOA["imd_rank"],col=colours_to_map,border = NA, reset = FALSE)
plot(st_geometry(roads),col="#6B6B6B",lwd=0.3, add=TRUE)
# Add legend
#legend("bottomleft", legend =c("a","b","c","d","e","f"), fill = my_colours)
legend("bottomleft", legend =breaks$brks, fill = my_colours)

breaks
## style: fisher
##   one of 20,811,575 possible partitions of this variable into 5 classes
##        [752,7058)      [7058,13109)   [13109,19201.5) [19201.5,26526.5) 
##                21                22                24                54 
##   [26526.5,32657] 
##                31

We will now add some points to the map by creating a new Spatial Points Data Frame as we demonstrated in the previous practical (see 2. Data Manipulation in R):

#Read in the location of schools
schools <- read.csv("./data/Milton_Keynes_SS_13.csv")
schools
##       URN                       SCHNAME    PCODE TOTPUPS AC5EM13 Easting
## 1  136468                Denbigh School  MK5 6EX    1388      80  483107
## 2  136844           The Hazeley Academy  MK8 0PT    1461      71  481650
## 3  110531              Lord Grey School  MK3 6EW    1464      54  485839
## 4  135665     The Milton Keynes Academy  MK6 5LA    1267      53  486120
## 5  136454               Oakgrove School MK10 9JQ    1249      65  488622
## 6  137052               Ousedale School MK16 0BJ    2195      78  486822
## 7  110532          The Radcliffe School MK12 5BT    1145      59  480805
## 8  110517     St Paul's Catholic School  MK6 5EN    1770      56  485623
## 9  136730      Shenley Brook End School  MK5 7ZT    1521      68  483160
## 10 138439      Sir Herbert Leon Academy  MK2 3HQ     676      49  487516
## 11 110526            Stantonbury Campus MK14 6BN    1984      53  484254
## 12 136842                   Walton High  MK7 7WH    1496      64  490091
## 13 110567 The Webber Independent School MK14 6DP     140      70  484790
##    Northing
## 1    236985
## 2    236327
## 3    234073
## 4    237569
## 5    238624
## 6    243183
## 7    240797
## 8    237130
## 9    235011
## 10   232076
## 11   241007
## 12   236608
## 13   241024

The new object “schools” contains 13 secondary schools within Milton Keynes, including their Easting and Northing, which are the coordinates of the schools in the British National Grid projection. Before you can make a spatial data frame, you need to check that there are no records with blank spatial references (i.e. Easting and Northing) - in this example, we use the subset() function.

The SpatialPointsDataFrame() function is then specified with the coordinates of the school “coords”, which are specified as matrix of values - cbind() is used to “column bind” the Easting and Northing lists together, i.e. so each row is a location. The “data” parameter specifies any attribute data - in this case we just use the original data frame. Finally, the “proj4string” is specified using the CRS() function. These are standard lookups to known as coordinate systems.

# Remove those schools without Easting or Northing
schools <- subset(schools, Easting != "" | Northing != "")

schools_SDF<-st_as_sf(schools, coords = c("Easting","Northing"),crs=27700)
# Create the SpatialPointsDataFrame
#schools_SDF <- SpatialPointsDataFrame(coords = cbind(schools$Easting, schools$Northing), data = schools, proj4string = CRS("+init=epsg:27700"))

We can now plot these locations on our map.

# Plot choropleth
plot(LSOA["imd_rank"],col=colours_to_map,border = NA, reset = FALSE)
plot(st_geometry(roads),col="#6B6B6B",lwd=0.3, add=TRUE, reset=FALSE)
plot(st_geometry(schools_SDF), pch = 19, cex = 1, col = "#442200",add=TRUE)

Plotting a subset of the map

Much in the same way that we can subset a data frame, we can also create subsets of the plot. For example, suppose we just wanted to view a map for a a specific Ward in Milton Keynes.

First we will read in the Ward boundaries.

WARD <- st_read("./data/england_cmwd_2011Polygon.shp")
## Reading layer `england_cmwd_2011Polygon' from data source 
##   `E:\Dropbox (Personal)\Auckland\Teaching\sem 1 2025\771\lab 1\Lab 1 _ 2024\2025\data\england_cmwd_2011Polygon.shp' 
##   using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
## Simple feature collection with 23 features and 4 fields
## Geometry type: POLYGON
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: 476285.3 ymin: 230902.3 xmax: 496530.4 ymax: 256158.2
## Projected CRS: OSGB36 / British National Grid

There are 23 wards, which are named as follows.

WARD$name
##  [1] "Bletchley and Fenny Stratford" "Bradwell"                     
##  [3] "Campbell Park"                 "Danesborough"                 
##  [5] "Denbigh"                       "Eaton Manor"                  
##  [7] "Emerson Valley"                "Furzton"                      
##  [9] "Hanslope Park"                 "Linford North"                
## [11] "Linford South"                 "Loughton Park"                
## [13] "Middleton"                     "Newport Pagnell North"        
## [15] "Newport Pagnell South"         "Olney"                        
## [17] "Sherington"                    "Stantonbury"                  
## [19] "Stony Stratford"               "Walton Park"                  
## [21] "Whaddon"                       "Wolverton"                    
## [23] "Woughton"

We can plot and label these as follows. The text() function applies text to the map, specifying three lists including the Easting, Northing and the text labels. The Easting and Northing are derived using the st_coordinates() function, which for spatial polygons takes the centre of the polygon extent if you specify st_centroid.

plot(st_geometry(WARD))
#plot(st_centroid(st_geometry(WARD)), add = TRUE, reset=FALSE)

text(st_coordinates(st_centroid(WARD)),WARD$name,cex = 0.5,offset = 0.5, adj = 1)
## Warning: st_centroid assumes attributes are constant over geometries

# it doesn't look great so we can move to some more sophisticated libraries

In the following example we can use the attributes of the spatial data frame to plot just a small area of the total simple feature data frame object. For example, to plot just Loughton Park we can use the square brackets to just select a single row that matches the name “Loughton Park”

plot(st_geometry(WARD[WARD$name == "Loughton Park",]))

This is a useful technique to create a more limited extent. We can use this as follows with some of the previous plots to create a “zoomed in map”. Note how we are building the map up as a series of layers.

plot(st_geometry(WARD[WARD$name == "Loughton Park",],border=NA, reset=FALSE)) #creates the extent, note that border = NA to make this polygon invisible
plot(LSOA["imd_rank"],col=colours_to_map,border = NA,add=TRUE, reset=FALSE) #plot the choropleth for the IMD
plot(st_geometry(roads),add=TRUE, col="#6B6B6B",lwd=0.3,reset=FALSE) #plot the roads
plot(st_geometry(schools_SDF), pch = 19, cex = 0.9, col = "#442200",add=TRUE) #plot the schools
text(st_coordinates(st_centroid(schools_SDF)),schools_SDF$SCHNAME,cex = 0.9,offset = 0.5, adj = 1)

Choropleth mapping in ggplot2

In the previous practical we introduce ggplot2 (see 5. Data Viz 1: Charts and Graphs), which can also be used to plot maps. The first stage is the extract the polygon boundaries from the spatial polygons/simple features object. We do this using the fortify() function; the “region” parameter is the attribute used to split the polygon - in this case the unique ID for each LSOA.

# Load the ggplot2 package
library(ggplot2)

We can now have a look at the new data frame object this created.

head(LSOA)
## Simple feature collection with 6 features and 12 fields
## Geometry type: POLYGON
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: 484809.9 ymin: 232500 xmax: 488372 ymax: 234990
## Projected CRS: Transverse_Mercator
##    LSOA11CD imd_rank imd_score income employment education health crime housing
## 1 E01016710    11648     23.67   0.20       0.14     28.57   0.47  0.36   17.34
## 2 E01016711    25604      8.87   0.10       0.07     10.62  -0.60 -0.76   19.87
## 3 E01016713    22671     11.27   0.09       0.09     18.38  -0.42 -0.57   18.40
## 4 E01016714     8671     29.07   0.24       0.15     37.17   0.44  0.29   22.76
## 5 E01016715    10843     25.04   0.17       0.14     31.33   0.33  0.79   25.15
## 6 E01016716    11207     24.39   0.19       0.14     24.94   0.57  0.35   18.58
##   living_env idaci idaopi                       geometry
## 1       2.35  0.25   0.23 POLYGON ((487797.2 233640.4...
## 2       2.31  0.17   0.07 POLYGON ((485678.5 233494.2...
## 3       7.72  0.14   0.08 POLYGON ((485628.2 233270.6...
## 4      19.28  0.31   0.22 POLYGON ((487329.3 233184.8...
## 5       3.82  0.22   0.16 POLYGON ((485228.1 233146.6...
## 6      14.52  0.25   0.16 POLYGON ((487502.1 234209.8...

You will see that the polygons have been split up into “groups” which refer to each of the LSOA codes - i.e. what you specified in the “region” attribute. The x and y are saved in the geometery column.

We can now use these attributes to create a choropleth map. First we setup the map using ggplot(). We can then tell ggplot how the map should look; firstly stating that the objects are geom_sf (simple features), and then we add some adjustment to the x and y labels, and alter the legend title (“+labs”).

Map<-ggplot(LSOA) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill=imd_rank))+labs(x = "Lat", y = "Long")
Map

We can also add layers to the plot as we did in the previous example. We can then add this to the “Map” object we just created, however we will build this up as layers. Rather than specifying LSOA in your initial ggplot brackets you specify it after geom(sf of the individual maps)

# Create choropleth

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data=LSOA, aes(fill=imd_rank))+labs(x = "Long", y = "Lat")+
    #geom_sf_text(aes(label = imd_rank), size = 2) +
  geom_sf(data=roads, color = "red")

   #geom_text_repel(aes(label = name, geometry = geometry),

We can add a further layer for the locations of the schools, and also adjust the color ramp.

# Create school plot

# Create choropleth

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data=LSOA, aes(fill=imd_rank))+labs(x = "Long", y = "Lat")+
    #geom_sf_text(aes(label = imd_rank), size = 2) +
  geom_sf(data=roads, color = "red") +
   #geom_text_repel(aes(label = name, geometry = geometry),
geom_sf(data = schools_SDF, aes(color=SCHNAME))

Have a look at some cool tutorials on how to use ggplot for sf objects. https://tmieno2.github.io/R-as-GIS-for-Economists/geom-sf.html https://learning.nceas.ucsb.edu/2020-02-RRCourse/spatial-vector-analysis-using-sf.html#visualize-with-ggplot https://geanders.github.io/navy_public_health/3-2-basic-mapping.html

It is also possible to control the other elements of the plot using “theme_bw()” which removes many of the visible elements.

#Create a clean plot
ggplot(LSOA) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = imd_rank)) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(fill = "Deprivation ranking") +
  scale_fill_continuous(low = "khaki", high =  "firebrick")+
geom_sf(data=roads, color="black",lwd = 0.1) +
#geom_text_repel(aes(label = name, geometry = geometry),
geom_sf(data = schools_SDF, aes(color=SCHNAME))

Making basic maps using tmap

A recent addition to R for creating maps is the tmap package. This is quite flexible and can be used to create various types of maps and geographic representations with relative simplicity. For someone starting with R, this is probably the simplest entry point. First we will install and load the package:

install.packages("tmap")
library(tmap)

We will first load a shapefile for the Leeds, UK.

#Read Shapefile
Leeds <- st_read("./data/E08000035.shp")
## Reading layer `E08000035' from data source 
##   `E:\Dropbox (Personal)\Auckland\Teaching\sem 1 2025\771\lab 1\Lab 1 _ 2024\2025\data\E08000035.shp' 
##   using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
## Simple feature collection with 482 features and 12 fields
## Geometry type: POLYGON
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: 413220.1 ymin: 422595.3 xmax: 446879 ymax: 450175.3
## Projected CRS: Transverse_Mercator
# Have a look at the attributes
head(Leeds)
## Simple feature collection with 6 features and 12 fields
## Geometry type: POLYGON
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: 416384.8 ymin: 440997.1 xmax: 421817.5 ymax: 443453.8
## Projected CRS: Transverse_Mercator
##    LSOA11CD imd_rank imd_score income employment education health crime housing
## 1 E01011264    18951     14.69   0.09       0.09     23.87   0.09 -0.48   16.54
## 2 E01011265    22149     11.71   0.08       0.06      7.89   0.12  0.05    9.92
## 3 E01011266    29793      5.46   0.02       0.03      1.01  -1.54 -0.35   27.38
## 4 E01011267     9622     27.18   0.16       0.13     31.45   0.91  0.48   23.35
## 5 E01011268     5862     35.74   0.19       0.19     49.35   1.03  0.51   22.10
## 6 E01011269    15025     19.03   0.12       0.10     22.35   0.22  0.30   13.25
##   living_env idaci idaopi                       geometry
## 1      22.99  0.14   0.08 POLYGON ((421326.9 442029, ...
## 2      29.35  0.08   0.14 POLYGON ((419139.6 441708.1...
## 3      17.77  0.04   0.03 POLYGON ((417620 443437.1, ...
## 4      24.62  0.21   0.18 POLYGON ((419400 442161.3, ...
## 5      32.85  0.19   0.26 POLYGON ((420161 441835.2, ...
## 6      30.23  0.12   0.18 POLYGON ((420546.8 441602.9...
#make sure your imd_rank is a numeric value
str(Leeds)
## Classes 'sf' and 'data.frame':   482 obs. of  13 variables:
##  $ LSOA11CD  : chr  "E01011264" "E01011265" "E01011266" "E01011267" ...
##  $ imd_rank  : num  18951 22149 29793 9622 5862 ...
##  $ imd_score : num  14.69 11.71 5.46 27.18 35.74 ...
##  $ income    : num  0.09 0.08 0.02 0.16 0.19 0.12 0.02 0.04 0.03 0.09 ...
##  $ employment: num  0.09 0.06 0.03 0.13 0.19 0.1 0.05 0.04 0.04 0.09 ...
##  $ education : num  23.87 7.89 1.01 31.45 49.35 ...
##  $ health    : num  0.09 0.12 -1.54 0.91 1.03 0.22 -0.68 -1.37 -0.73 0.44 ...
##  $ crime     : num  -0.48 0.05 -0.35 0.48 0.51 0.3 -0.63 -0.38 -0.94 0.09 ...
##  $ housing   : num  16.54 9.92 27.38 23.35 22.1 ...
##  $ living_env: num  23 29.4 17.8 24.6 32.9 ...
##  $ idaci     : num  0.14 0.08 0.04 0.21 0.19 0.12 0.01 0.04 0.02 0.08 ...
##  $ idaopi    : num  0.08 0.14 0.03 0.18 0.26 0.18 0.03 0.06 0.05 0.17 ...
##  $ geometry  :sfc_POLYGON of length 482; first list element: List of 1
##   ..$ : num [1:833, 1:2] 421327 421323 421323 421321 421321 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:3] "XY" "POLYGON" "sfg"
##  - attr(*, "sf_column")= chr "geometry"
##  - attr(*, "agr")= Factor w/ 3 levels "constant","aggregate",..: NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
##   ..- attr(*, "names")= chr [1:12] "LSOA11CD" "imd_rank" "imd_score" "income" ...

We will store the map in the “M” object - first we setup the map and projection - note that we use the CRS 27700 which is the code for the British National Grid:

m <- tm_shape(Leeds, projection=27700)
## 
## ── tmap v3 code detected ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## [v3->v4] `tm_shape()`: use `crs` instead of `projection`.

Next we can add a style to the map - this includes the variable to use for coloring (“col=”), in this case, the rank of the area by the Index of Multiple Deprivation. The style is set as “equal” which relates to how the IMD ranks are broken up into color bins; the border color (“border.col=”) and transparency (” border.alpha =“); title and also pallet for the choropleth (”palette = “) which uses the colorbrewer pallets. Finally, the”showNA” option can be set as true or false and relates to whether missing values are shown in the legend.

# commented out are v3 tmap maps :)
#m <- tm_shape(Leeds, projection=27700) +
#   tm_polygons(col="imd_score", style="equal",n=5,border.col = "grey50",  border.alpha = .5, title="IMD Quintile", showNA=FALSE,palette="Blues")
#Print plot
#m
m<-tm_shape(Leeds, crs=27700) +
  tm_polygons(
    fill = "imd_score",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(
      values = "brewer.reds"))
m

We can then add some final options with tm_layout() to remove the frame around the plot:

m<-tm_shape(Leeds, crs=27700) +
  tm_polygons(
    fill = "imd_score",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(
      values = "brewer.blues"))+
  tm_layout(
    frame = FALSE, 
    legend.frame = FALSE)

#Print plot
m

As with the ggplot2 and base R maps, it is also possible to build up layers and use lines or points to add context to the maps. Here we replicate the map shown earlier:

tm_shape(LSOA, crs = 27700) +
    tm_polygons(fill = "imd_score",fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.greys"),col_alpha = .5, fill.legend = tm_legend("IMD Rank"))+
  
# Add roads
  tm_shape(roads) +
    tm_lines(lwd.scale = 2, col = "grey50",col_alpha = 0.5)  +
    tm_add_legend(type="lines", col="grey50", title="Roads") +
#Add schools
#Add schools
tm_shape(schools_SDF) +
    tm_symbols(size=0.5,shape=20, fill = "#f7756d") +
    tm_add_legend(type="symbols", shape=20, fill ="#f7756d", title="Schools") +
#Remove the frame and set legend position
    tm_layout(legend.outside=TRUE, frame = FALSE, component.autoscale = FALSE, legend.frame=FALSE)

Alternative types of map

So far we have only considered creating contextual choropleth maps, however, tmap is also useful to create a number of alternative map types including proportional symbol maps and cartograms.

First we will import a file of ward centroids which has a “Crimes” attribute that records the number of recorded crimes within the ward during August 2016.

# Read in crime data
Crimes_SP <- st_read("./data/crimes.shp")
## Reading layer `crimes' from data source 
##   `E:\Dropbox (Personal)\Auckland\Teaching\sem 1 2025\771\lab 1\Lab 1 _ 2024\2025\data\crimes.shp' 
##   using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
## Simple feature collection with 33 features and 2 fields
## Geometry type: POINT
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: 417851.1 ymin: 425803.3 xmax: 442841.9 ymax: 445534.9
## Projected CRS: Transverse_Mercator
# Read in crime data; and set crimes as a numeric
str(Crimes_SP)
## Classes 'sf' and 'data.frame':   33 obs. of  3 variables:
##  $ Zone    : chr  "00DAGL" "00DAGM" "00DAGN" "00DAGP" ...
##  $ Crimes  : num  114 143 152 516 435 ...
##  $ geometry:sfc_POINT of length 33; first list element:  'XY' num  425940 442832
##  - attr(*, "sf_column")= chr "geometry"
##  - attr(*, "agr")= Factor w/ 3 levels "constant","aggregate",..: NA NA
##   ..- attr(*, "names")= chr [1:2] "Zone" "Crimes"
# if crimes are not numeric change the character to num
Crimes_SP$Crimes <- as.numeric(as.character(Crimes_SP$Crimes))

The following code first creates the choropleth with tm_polygons(), and then appends the bubbles using tm_bubbles() based on the “Crimes” attribute.

tm_shape(Leeds, crs = 27700) +
    tm_polygons(fill = "imd_score", fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(values = "brewer.greys",5,style = "equal"), col_alpha = .5, fill.legend = tm_legend("IMD Rank"))+
    # Add scaled bubbles
tm_shape(Crimes_SP) +
    tm_bubbles("Crimes",col="blue", title.size = "Crimes")+ 

  # Add legend and remove borders
tm_layout(frame = FALSE,legend.text.size = 0.65)
## 
## ── tmap v3 code detected ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## [v3->v4] `tm_bubbles()`: use 'fill' for the fill color of polygons/symbols
## (instead of 'col'), and 'col' for the outlines (instead of 'border.col').
## This message is displayed once every 8 hours.

A further type of map that tmap can create is a cartogram; which is actually imported from the cartogram package. This type of representation scales each of the zones by an absolute measure such as population. Thus, zones with low counts become smaller and high counts larger. There are a lot of algorithms to make cartograms, however, the implementation within tmap is the commonly used continuous area cartogram.

We will therefore import some population data and append these to the spatial polygons data frame created earlier called “Leeds”:

#Read population data
Leeds_pop <- read.csv("./data/Leeds_Census_2011.csv")

#Join to spatial polygon dataframe
Leeds <- merge(Leeds, Leeds_pop, by.x = "LSOA11CD", by.y = "Code")

The following code creates the cartogram, however, takes a long time to run. If using this function, then this time increases the more complex and large the set of polygons. An alternative but free non R solution is ScapeToad.

library(cartogram)
Leeds_Cartogram <- cartogram_cont(Leeds, "Usual_Residents_2011", itermax = 15, maxSizeError = 1.0001,prepare = "adjust", threshold = 0.05) # Takes a long time to process

We can now create a map as we did a basic choropleth, however, you will see that the zones are distorted:

Further resources / training

  • An Introduction to R for Spatial Analysis and Mapping - a great primer on mapping in R
  • ggplot2 book - code and sample chapters for the book
  • Scape Toad - A great tool for creating cartograms
